# PRD: StoryVerse AI Studio

**One prompt becomes an entire creative universe — story, characters, art, music, narration, and marketing, generated by a coordinated team of AI agents.**

| | |
|---|---|
| **Owner** | Solo developer |
| **Status** | Draft |
| **Timeline** | 2-week hackathon sprint (MVP) → full vision as post-hackathon roadmap |
| **Document version** | 1.0 |

---

## 1. Summary

StoryVerse AI Studio turns a single line of user input — e.g. *"An abandoned city where robots dream"* — into a complete, cohesive creative package: story, character cards, world-building, concept art, a poster, a narrated trailer script, background music, and a social media promo pack. It does this through a multi-agent AI pipeline (orchestrated with LangGraph) rather than a single prompt-to-output call, which is what makes the output feel like a coordinated creative studio instead of a generic AI generator.

## 2. Problem Statement

Creators regularly have a spark of an idea but turning that spark into a shareable, presentable creative package (story + visuals + audio + promotional material) takes days or weeks and requires skills across writing, illustration, sound design, and marketing. Existing AI tools solve pieces of this (text generation, image generation, voice synthesis) in isolation — none of them stitch the pieces into one coherent universe automatically.

## 3. Goals

- Demonstrate AI as a **creative partner**, not just a content generator.
- Show genuine **multi-agent orchestration** where each agent has a distinct role and passes context to the next.
- Combine **text, image, and audio** into a single connected output (multimodal).
- Produce a demo-able "wow moment": one prompt in, a full creative launch package out, in under 2 minutes.
- Ship a working, polished MVP solo within 2 weeks that satisfies hackathon judging criteria.

## 4. Non-Goals (Out of Scope)

- User authentication / accounts
- Real-time multi-user collaboration
- AI-generated video (full video synthesis)
- Fine-tuning custom models
- Complex relational database / multi-tenant data model
- Mobile app
- Payments / billing

## 5. Target User

Solo creators, game designers, writers, and hobbyists who want to rapidly prototype a story universe (for a pitch, a game concept, a comic, or personal creative exploration) without assembling a team.

## 6. Core Product Experience

1. User enters a single creative prompt.
2. The system visibly shows a pipeline of AI agents running in sequence (for demo transparency and "wow" factor).
3. Output streams in progressively: story → characters → world → images → audio → trailer script → marketing pack.
4. User can view, and eventually download, the complete "Creative Package."

---

## 7. Full Product Vision (Multi-Agent Architecture)

The full vision is a chain of 8 specialized agents, each responsible for one creative discipline, coordinated by LangGraph so that later agents receive the output of earlier ones as context.

| Agent | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| 🧠 **Idea Agent** | Expands a rough concept into a polished story premise |
| ✍️ **Writer Agent** | Creates characters, plot, dialogue, and scenes |
| 🧬 **Character Agent** | Builds detailed character cards and relationships |
| 🌍 **World Builder Agent** | Generates world map, background lore, setting rules |
| 🎨 **Visual Agent** | Generates concept art, posters, and scene illustrations |
| 🎵 **Audio Agent** | Creates ambient soundscapes and soundtrack suggestions (builds on existing SoundWeaver work) |
| 🎬 **Director Agent** | Produces storyboards, camera directions, and a trailer script |
| 📣 **Marketing Agent** | Generates social posts, thumbnails, and promotional copy |

### 7.1 Full Agent Pipeline

```
User Prompt
    │
    ▼
Idea Agent
    │
    ▼
Story Agent (Writer)
    │
    ▼
Character Agent
    │
    ▼
World Builder Agent
    │
    ▼
Visual Prompt Generator → Image API (Flux2 / Flux Kontext via Replicate)
    │
    ▼
Audio Prompt Generator → Music API (ElevenLabs Music)
    │
    ▼
Narration Agent → Voice API (ElevenLabs)
    │
    ▼
Director Agent → Storyboard
    │
    ▼
Marketing Agent
    │
    ▼
Final Creative Package
```

### 7.2 System Architecture

```
React / Next.js (Frontend)
        │
   FastAPI Backend
        │
 ┌──────┼──────────────┐
 │      │               │
LangGraph  Granite LLM   Asset APIs
 │      │               │
Agents  Story/Character  Images / Music / Voice
 (all 8)  Generation
        │
        ▼
 Final Creative Package
```

### 7.3 Full-Vision Feature List (Post-MVP Roadmap)

- Full 8-agent pipeline with visible agent-by-agent progress UI (React Flow)
- Trailer assembly (storyboard + voice + music + scene images stitched via MoviePy/FFmpeg)
- Persistent project storage (Supabase) so users can revisit past universes
- Exportable, shareable "Creative Package" bundle
- Expanded world-building (maps, timelines, factions)

---

## 8. MVP Scope (2-Week Solo Build)

To ship something demoable and complete within 2 weeks solo, the MVP narrows the full vision to the following:

### 8.1 MVP Features

1. User enters a creative prompt (e.g., *"A sci-fi mystery set in Hyderabad"*)
2. Generate a story outline
3. Generate character profiles
4. Generate world-building details
5. Create 3–5 concept images
6. Generate a movie poster
7. Produce a narrated trailer script with background music
8. Export everything as a downloadable "Creative Package"

### 8.2 MVP Agent Chain

The MVP runs a condensed version of the full pipeline — Idea → Writer → Character → World Builder → Visual → Audio → Narration → Director (trailer script only, no assembled video) → Marketing (lightweight) — using the same LangGraph orchestration pattern so it extends cleanly into the full vision post-hackathon.

### 8.3 Explicitly Deferred from MVP

- Full agent-by-agent visual pipeline UI (nice-to-have only if time allows)
- Video assembly / stitched trailer (MoviePy/FFmpeg) — script + assets only for MVP
- Persistent multi-project storage — single-session generation is sufficient
- Any items listed under Section 4 (Non-Goals)

---

## 9. Technical Design

### 9.1 Tech Stack

| Layer | Choice | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Frontend | Next.js 15 (React), TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, Framer Motion | Fast to build, polished, demo-ready |
| Frontend (optional) | React Flow | Visualizes the agent workflow live |
| Backend | FastAPI, Python 3.11+, Uvicorn | Strong fit for AI API orchestration |
| AI Orchestration | LangGraph + LangChain | True multi-agent coordination, not a single prompt |
| LLM | IBM Granite (Granite 4 / Granite 3.3 Instruct) via watsonx.ai | Story generation, brainstorming, dialogue, planning, scene creation |
| Images | Flux2 / Flux Kontext via Replicate API (Hugging Face token available) | Concept art, posters, scene illustrations |
| Voice Narration | ElevenLabs | Narrated trailer script |
| Background Music | ElevenLabs Music | Soundtrack generation |
| Sound Effects | Freesound API | Builds on existing SoundWeaver integration |
| Storage | AWS S3 | Images, audio, video assets |
| Database | SQLite or Supabase | Lightweight project/story/character/asset storage |
| Deployment | Vercel (frontend) + Render or Railway (backend) | Fast, low-ops deployment for a solo dev |
| Dev tooling | IBM Bob (development) | Mandatory per hackathon requirements |

### 9.2 Folder Structure

```
storyverse-ai/
  frontend/
    app/
    components/
    pages/

  backend/
    agents/
      idea.py
      writer.py
      character.py
      world.py
      visual.py
      director.py
      marketing.py
    api/
    services/
      granite.py
      image_api.py
      audio_api.py
      voice_api.py
    assets/
    database/
    utils/

  README.md
```

### 9.3 Data Model (Lightweight)

Stored per project:
- **Projects** — prompt, timestamps, status
- **Stories** — outline, plot, scenes
- **Characters** — profile, relationships
- **Assets** — image URLs, audio URLs, video URLs (S3-hosted)

### 9.4 APIs Needed

| Purpose | API |
|---|---|
| Story generation | IBM Granite (watsonx.ai) |
| AI orchestration | LangGraph |
| Images | Flux2 / Flux Kontext via Replicate |
| Voice narration | ElevenLabs |
| Background music | ElevenLabs Music API |
| Sound effects | Freesound API |
| Storage | AWS S3 |

---

## 10. Success Metrics

- **Functional completeness**: all 8 MVP features (Section 8.1) work end-to-end without manual intervention.
- **Demo speed**: full pipeline completes in ~90 seconds for a live demo.
- **Output coherence**: characters, art, and lore generated are consistent with each other (not contradictory).
- **Judge reaction**: qualitative "wow" response during live demo.

## 11. Risks & Mitigations

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| API latency across 6–8 chained calls blows past demo time budget | Parallelize independent agent calls where possible (e.g., image + audio generation); cache/pre-warm during demo |
| Image/audio API rate limits or costs during testing | Use lower-cost/test-tier generations during development, upgrade only for final demo runs |
| Granite output inconsistency between agents (e.g., character names drifting) | Pass structured JSON context between agents rather than free text |
| Solo dev time constraints | Strict adherence to MVP scope (Section 8); full vision features are explicitly deferred |
| Video assembly complexity (MoviePy/FFmpeg) | Deferred from MVP; trailer delivered as script + assets only |

---

# Hackathon-Specific Details

## H.1 Mandatory Requirement: IBM Bob

Per hackathon rules, **IBM Bob must be used during development**. This is a development-tool requirement, separate from the product's own AI stack (which uses IBM Granite via watsonx.ai for generation).

## H.2 Judging Criteria Alignment

| Criterion | How StoryVerse AI Studio addresses it |
|---|---|
| **Technical execution** | Real multi-agent orchestration via LangGraph, not a single prompt; structured hand-offs between agents |
| **Innovation** | Combines text, image, and audio generation into one connected creative pipeline — most tools solve one modality at a time |
| **Challenge fit** | Directly matches "AI as a creative partner" theme rather than a plain content generator |
| **Feasibility** | Stack is realistic for a solo developer in 2 weeks (mature APIs, no custom model training) |
| **Real-world impact** | Solves a genuine, time-consuming creative bottleneck (weeks of work → minutes) |

## H.3 Demo Script (Target: ~90 seconds)

1. **Hook (5s)**: "Creators have ideas. Turning them into a full creative universe takes weeks. We do it in 90 seconds."
2. **Input (5s)**: Type prompt live — e.g. *"Pirates in Space"*.
3. **Pipeline visualization (60–70s)**: Show agents firing in sequence (ideally visualized via React Flow) as each output streams in — story outline, character cards, concept art, poster, narrated trailer line, background music.
4. **Reveal (10s)**: Full "Creative Package" assembled on screen — story, characters, art, poster, narrated trailer script with music.
5. **Close (5s)**: One-line pitch — "One prompt. A full Netflix-quality creative package."

## H.4 Bobcoin Budget Strategy

**Allocation**: 60 Bobcoins (vs. the hackathon-standard 40, which IBM sizes for a "compelling proof-of-concept"). This gives ~50% more runway than baseline — but the full-stack surface area of this project (FastAPI + Next.js + LangGraph + 5 external APIs + S3) is larger than a typical PoC, so budget discipline matters.

**Key fact**: Bobcoins are consumed based on interaction complexity and context size, not a flat per-feature rate. Long sessions, broad folder/`@terminal` context dumps, and repeated debugging loops are the main cost drivers — not the number of features built.

### Where to spend Bob (high-leverage)
- LangGraph agent orchestration and state-passing logic between agents
- API integration glue code (Granite/watsonx, Replicate, ElevenLabs, Freesound, S3)
- Backend architecture scaffolding (FastAPI routes, service layer structure)
- Debugging agent-chain context/consistency issues

### Where to conserve coins (do manually or with lighter tools)
- Frontend UI styling and layout polish (Tailwind/shadcn — largely templated)
- Config files, environment setup, boilerplate CRUD
- README, documentation, non-code assets

### Session hygiene rules
1. Start a fresh Bob chat per task — don't carry old context (bug fix, refactor, docs = 3 separate chats, not 1)
2. Use exact file/line mentions (`@/backend/agents/writer.py:20-45`) instead of whole-folder or `@terminal` dumps
3. Avoid connecting broad MCP tool surfaces you don't need for the task at hand — tool definitions eat context before the first real question
4. Ask cheap, scoped questions before big ones (e.g. `git diff --stat` before a full diff dump)

### Agent build sequencing (one Bob task per agent)

Each of the 8 agents is a bounded unit (own prompt, own input/output contract, own file), which makes them a natural fit for one Bob task each — but don't start every agent task fully cold. Sequence it as:

1. **Task 1 — Shared scaffolding**: LangGraph graph skeleton, shared state schema (what gets passed between agents), Pydantic models for story/character/world objects, base agent pattern if used. This is built once and referenced by every agent task that follows.
2. **Tasks 2–9 — One task per agent**: Idea → Writer → Character → World Builder → Visual → Audio → Director → Marketing. Each task is scoped to its own file (`agents/idea.py`, `agents/writer.py`, etc.) and references the scaffolding files by exact mention (e.g. `@/backend/models/schemas.py`) rather than re-explaining the architecture from scratch each time.
3. **Close each agent's task once it works and is tested.** Don't reopen a finished agent's chat to bolt on unrelated fixes later — open a new, narrowly-scoped task instead. Reopening old sessions is what causes the context pileup that quietly burns Bobcoins.

This keeps each session's context small (cheap) while avoiding the cost of re-deriving shared conventions in every single agent task.

### Budget checkpoints (14-day sprint)

| Day | Target Bobcoin usage | Action if over budget |
|---|---|---|
| Day 5–6 (~35% through) | ≤ 40% spent | On track — continue as planned |
| Day 5–6 (~35% through) | > 50% spent | Cut scope immediately: drop any full-vision stretch goals, lock to MVP-only (Section 8) |
| Day 10 (~70% through) | ≤ 75% spent | Proceed to polish/demo prep |
| Day 10 (~70% through) | > 85% spent | Freeze Bob usage for new features; reserve remaining coins for critical bug fixes only, finish remaining work manually |

### Fallback if coins run out early
Bob is a development-tool requirement, not a runtime dependency of the product — if Bobcoins are exhausted before day 14, development can continue manually (you keep coding without Bob), but ensure all Bob-assisted work up to that point is captured in the required `bob_sessions` exports, since the submission is judged on demonstrated Bob usage, not on using it for 100% of the build.

## H.5 Two-Week Solo Build Plan (High-Level)

| Days | Focus |
|---|---|
| 1–2 | Backend scaffold (FastAPI), LangGraph pipeline skeleton, Granite integration |
| 3–5 | Idea, Writer, Character, World Builder agents (text pipeline complete) |
| 6–8 | Visual Agent + Replicate (Flux2/Flux Kontext) integration |
| 9–10 | Audio Agent + ElevenLabs (voice + music) integration |
| 11–12 | Frontend build (Next.js UI, streaming output, optional React Flow visualization) |
| 13 | Marketing Agent, Creative Package export, end-to-end testing |
| 14 | Polish, demo rehearsal, buffer for API flakiness |

## H.6 Explicitly Skipped for Hackathon (Time-Boxing)

- User authentication
- Real-time collaboration
- AI video generation
- Custom model fine-tuning
- Complex databases
- Multiplayer editing
- Mobile app
- Payment integration

---

## 12. Open Questions

- Confirm final choice between SQLite and Supabase for MVP persistence.
- Confirm whether React Flow agent-visualization UI is in-scope for MVP or a stretch goal given the 2-week timeline.
- Confirm ElevenLabs usage tier/quota is sufficient for both development iteration and live demo.
